subject: Chemistry
q: Determine the empirical formula of Kevlar, used in making bullet proof vests, is 70.6% C, 4.2% H, 11.8% N and 13.4% O:
question: Determine the empirical formula of Kevlar, used in making bullet proof vests, is 70.6% C, 4.2% H, 11.8% N and 13.4% O:
solution:
Take $100\text{ g}$ of Kevlar.

Then the masses of elements are:

  • Carbon $= 70.6\text{ g}$

  • Hydrogen $= 4.2\text{ g}$

  • Nitrogen $= 11.8\text{ g}$

  • Oxygen $= 13.4\text{ g}$

Now change these masses into moles:

$$ \text{Moles of C}=\frac{70.6}{12}=5.883 $$

$$ \text{Moles of H}=\frac{4.2}{1}=4.2 $$

$$ \text{Moles of N}=\frac{11.8}{14}=0.843 $$

$$ \text{Moles of O}=\frac{13.4}{16}=0.8375 $$

Now divide all by the smallest value, which is $0.8375$:

$$ \text{C}=\frac{5.883}{0.8375}\approx 7.02 $$

$$ \text{H}=\frac{4.2}{0.8375}\approx 5.01 $$

$$ \text{N}=\frac{0.843}{0.8375}\approx 1.01 $$

$$ \text{O}=\frac{0.8375}{0.8375}=1 $$

So the simplest whole number ratio is

$$ \text{C}:\text{H}:\text{N}:\text{O} = 7:5:1:1 $$

Hence the empirical formula is

$$ \boxed{C_7H_5NO} $$

So the correct option is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

subject: Chemistry
q: Suppose two elements X and Y combine to form two compounds XY2 and X2Y3. If 0.05 mole of XY2 weighs 5 g while $3.011 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of X2Y3 weighs 85 g, then atomic masses of X and Y are respectively :
question: Suppose two elements X and Y combine to form two compounds XY2 and X2Y3. If 0.05 mole of XY2 weighs 5 g while $3.011 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of X2Y3 weighs 85 g, then atomic masses of X and Y are respectively :
solution:
From the given data, first find the molar masses of the two compounds.

For $XY_2$:

$0.05$ mole weighs $5 \, \text{g}$

So, $1$ mole will weigh

$$\frac{5}{0.05} = 100 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

So molar mass of $XY_2 = 100$.

That means

$$X + 2Y = 100 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Now for $X_2Y_3$:

$3.011 \times 10^{23}$ molecules is half of Avogadro number, so it is $0.5$ mole.

Given $0.5$ mole weighs $85 \, \text{g}$

So, $1$ mole will weigh

$$\frac{85}{0.5} = 170 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

So molar mass of $X_2Y_3 = 170$.

That gives

$$2X + 3Y = 170 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Now solve equations (1) and (2):

From (1),

$$X = 100 - 2Y$$

Put this in (2):

$$2(100 - 2Y) + 3Y = 170$$

$$200 - 4Y + 3Y = 170$$

$$200 - Y = 170$$

$$Y = 30$$

Now,

$$X = 100 - 2(30) = 40$$

So the atomic masses are

$$X = 40,\quad Y = 30$$

Option C: $40, 30$

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of atoms in 560g of Fe (Fe at. mass = 56g mol−1) is :-
question: The number of atoms in 560g of Fe (Fe at. mass = 56g mol−1) is :-
solution:
For iron,

$$\text{Moles of Fe}=\frac{560}{56}=10\ \text{mol}$$

Number of atoms in $560\,\text{g}$ Fe $=10N_A$

Now check the options.

For $70\,\text{g}$ nitrogen:

Atomic mass of $N=14\,\text{g mol}^{-1}$

$$\text{Moles of N atoms}=\frac{70}{14}=5\ \text{mol}$$

Number of atoms in $70\,\text{g}$ N $=5N_A$

So, number of atoms in $560\,\text{g}$ Fe is

$$10N_A = 2\times 5N_A$$

So it is twice that of $70\,\text{g}$ N.

Now for $20\,\text{g}$ hydrogen:

Atomic mass of $H=1\,\text{g mol}^{-1}$

$$\text{Moles of H atoms}=\frac{20}{1}=20\ \text{mol}$$

Number of atoms in $20\,\text{g}$ H $=20N_A$

Half of this is

$$\frac{1}{2}\times 20N_A=10N_A$$

This is equal to the number of atoms in $560\,\text{g}$ Fe.

So both statements are correct.

$$\boxed{\text{Option C: both are correct}}$$

subject: Chemistry
q: Caffeine has a molecular weight of 194. If it contains 29% by mass of nitrogen, number of atoms of nitrogen in one molecule of caffeine is :-
question: Caffeine has a molecular weight of 194. If it contains 29% by mass of nitrogen, number of atoms of nitrogen in one molecule of caffeine is :-
solution:
Take the molecular mass of caffeine as $194$.

Nitrogen is $29\%$ by mass, so mass of nitrogen in one mole of caffeine is

$$ \frac{29}{100} \times 194 = 56.26 $$

This is about $56$ g, and atomic mass of nitrogen is $14$.

So number of nitrogen atoms in one molecule will be

$$ \frac{56}{14} = 4 $$

So caffeine contains $4$ nitrogen atoms.

Option C: $4$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which concentration unit is not affected by temperature :
question: Which concentration unit is not affected by temperature :
solution:
The correct answer is Option D: Molality.

Molality is defined as the number of moles of solute present in $1\,\text{kg}$ of solvent.

$$ \text{Molality} = \frac{\text{moles of solute}}{\text{mass of solvent in kg}} $$

Since it depends on mass, and mass does not change with temperature, molality remains unchanged when temperature changes.

But normality, formality, and molarity are based on volume of solution.

For example,

$$ \text{Molarity} = \frac{\text{moles of solute}}{\text{volume of solution in litre}} $$

Volume changes with temperature, so these concentration units change with temperature.

So, the concentration unit not affected by temperature is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Molality}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: The molality of a solution prepared by dissolving 125 mL of pure methanol ($d = 0.8 \; \text{g mL}^{-1}$) in 375 g of ethanol is :
question: The molality of a solution prepared by dissolving 125 mL of pure methanol ($d = 0.8 \; \text{g mL}^{-1}$) in 375 g of ethanol is :
solution:
First find the mass of methanol.

Given:

  • Volume of methanol $= 125 \text{ mL}$

  • Density of methanol $= 0.8 \text{ g mL}^{-1}$

So,

$$ \text{Mass of methanol} = \text{density} \times \text{volume} $$

$$ = 0.8 \times 125 = 100 \text{ g} $$

Now find moles of methanol.

Molar mass of methanol, $CH_3OH$:

$$ 12 + 4(1) + 16 = 32 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

So,

$$ \text{Moles of methanol} = \frac{100}{32} = 3.125 \text{ mol} $$

Mass of solvent ethanol $= 375 \text{ g} = 0.375 \text{ kg}$

Now use the formula of molality:

$$ m = \frac{\text{moles of solute}}{\text{mass of solvent in kg}} $$

$$ m = \frac{3.125}{0.375} = 8.33 $$

So the molality of the solution is

$$ \boxed{8.33 \text{ mol kg}^{-1}} $$

Correct option: C

subject: Chemistry
q:
H2SO4 can be prepared in following two step process: [% yield of each step is indicated]

(A) $\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{50 \% \text { yield }} \mathrm{SO}_2$ (g)

(B) $$ \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{SO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\ell) \xrightarrow{80 \% \text { yield }} \\ \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \text { (aq.) }+\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g}) $$

What mass of sulphur is required to prepare 1960 gm H2SO4?
question:
H2SO4 can be prepared in following two step process: [% yield of each step is indicated]

(A) $\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{50 \% \text { yield }} \mathrm{SO}_2$ (g)

(B) $$ \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{SO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\ell) \xrightarrow{80 \% \text { yield }} \\ \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \text { (aq.) }+\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{~g}) $$

What mass of sulphur is required to prepare 1960 gm H2SO4?
solution:
Given mass of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ prepared is $1960 \text{ g}$.

Molar mass of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ is

$$ 2(1)+32+4(16)=98 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

So, moles of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ required are

$$ \frac{1960}{98}=20 \text{ mol} $$

Now look at reaction (B):

$$ \mathrm{NO_2 + SO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 + NO} $$

From the balanced reaction,

$$ 1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{SO_2} \rightarrow 1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{H_2SO_4} $$

But the yield of this step is only $80\%$.

So, to actually get $20$ mol of $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$, the theoretical amount should be

$$ \frac{20 \times 100}{80}=25 \text{ mol} $$

So, we need $25$ mol of $\mathrm{SO_2}$ from step (A).

Now reaction (A) is:

$$ \mathrm{S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2} $$

Here,

$$ 1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{S} \rightarrow 1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{SO_2} $$

But the yield of this step is $50\%$.

So, to actually get $25$ mol of $\mathrm{SO_2}$, sulphur required theoretically is

$$ \frac{25 \times 100}{50}=50 \text{ mol} $$

Molar mass of sulphur is $32 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

So, mass of sulphur required is

$$ 50 \times 32 = 1600 \text{ g} $$

Hence, the required mass of sulphur is

$$ \boxed{1600 \text{ g}} $$

Correct option: C.

subject: Chemistry
q:
A compound contains 69.5% oxygen and 30.5% nitrogen and its molecular weight is 92.
The formula of that compound is :
question:
A compound contains 69.5% oxygen and 30.5% nitrogen and its molecular weight is 92.
The formula of that compound is :
solution:
Take $100\ \text{g}$ of the compound.

So, the masses of nitrogen and oxygen are:

$$ \text{Mass of N} = 30.5\ \text{g} $$

$$ \text{Mass of O} = 69.5\ \text{g} $$

Now find the number of moles of each element.

For nitrogen:

$$ \text{Moles of N}=\frac{30.5}{14}=2.18 $$

For oxygen:

$$ \text{Moles of O}=\frac{69.5}{16}=4.34 $$

Now divide both by the smaller value, $2.18$.

$$ \text{N}=\frac{2.18}{2.18}=1 $$

$$ \text{O}=\frac{4.34}{2.18}\approx 2 $$

So, the empirical formula is:

$$ NO_2 $$

Empirical formula mass of $NO_2$ is:

$$ 14 + 2(16) = 46 $$

Given molecular mass is $92$.

$$ \text{Multiplying factor}=\frac{92}{46}=2 $$

So, molecular formula is:

$$ (NO_2)_2 = N_2O_4 $$

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{N_2O_4} $$

Option C is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: In sea water, the most abundant dissolved element is chlorine, present in the amount 19 g per kg of sea water. The total volume of Earth’s oceans is $1.4 \times 10^{21}$ L. Calculate the number of g-atoms of chlorine that can be obtained from the oceans. Take density of sea water as 1 g/mL ($N_A = 6 \times 10^{23}$).
question: In sea water, the most abundant dissolved element is chlorine, present in the amount 19 g per kg of sea water. The total volume of Earth’s oceans is $1.4 \times 10^{21}$ L. Calculate the number of g-atoms of chlorine that can be obtained from the oceans. Take density of sea water as 1 g/mL ($N_A = 6 \times 10^{23}$).
solution:
Given concentration of chlorine in sea water:

$$19 \text{ g of Cl per kg of sea water}$$

Volume of oceans:

$$1.4 \times 10^{21} \text{ L}$$

Density of sea water is $1 \text{ g/mL}$.

Since,

$$1 \text{ L} = 1000 \text{ mL}$$

So mass of sea water is:

$$1.4 \times 10^{21} \times 1000 \text{ g}$$

$$= 1.4 \times 10^{24} \text{ g}$$

Converting into kg:

$$1.4 \times 10^{24} \text{ g} = 1.4 \times 10^{21} \text{ kg}$$

Now, chlorine present is $19 \text{ g}$ per kg of sea water.

So total mass of chlorine is:

$$19 \times 1.4 \times 10^{21} \text{ g}$$

$$= 26.6 \times 10^{21} \text{ g}$$

$$= 2.66 \times 10^{22} \text{ g}$$

Now, gram-atoms of chlorine means moles of chlorine atoms.

Atomic mass of chlorine:

$$35.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

So,

$$\text{Gram-atoms of chlorine} = \frac{2.66 \times 10^{22}}{35.5}$$

$$= 7.5 \times 10^{20}$$

So the number of g-atoms of chlorine potentially available from oceans is:

$$\boxed{7.5 \times 10^{20}}$$

Correct option: Option A.

subject: Chemistry
q: Mixture of MgCO3 & NaHCO3 on strong heating give CO2 & H2O in 3 : 1 mole ratio. The weight % of NaHCO3 present in the mixture is :
question: Mixture of MgCO3 & NaHCO3 on strong heating give CO2 & H2O in 3 : 1 mole ratio. The weight % of NaHCO3 present in the mixture is :
solution:
On strong heating,

$$ \mathrm{MgCO_3 \rightarrow MgO + CO_2} $$

So, $1$ mole of $\mathrm{MgCO_3}$ gives $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CO_2}$.

Also,

$$ 2\mathrm{NaHCO_3 \rightarrow Na_2CO_3 + CO_2 + H_2O} $$

So, $2$ moles of $\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$ give $1$ mole of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ and $1$ mole of $\mathrm{H_2O}$.

Let the moles of $\mathrm{MgCO_3}$ be $x$ and the moles of $\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$ be $y$.

Moles of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ formed:

$$ x + \frac{y}{2} $$

Moles of $\mathrm{H_2O}$ formed:

$$ \frac{y}{2} $$

Given ratio:

$$ \frac{\mathrm{CO_2}}{\mathrm{H_2O}} = \frac{3}{1} $$

So,

$$ \frac{x + \frac{y}{2}}{\frac{y}{2}} = 3 $$

$$ x + \frac{y}{2} = \frac{3y}{2} $$

$$ x = y $$

So, moles of $\mathrm{MgCO_3}$ and $\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$ are equal.

Now,

$$ Molar\ mass\ of\ MgCO_3 = 24 + 12 + 48 = 84\ \mathrm{g/mol} $$

$$ Molar\ mass\ of\ NaHCO_3 = 23 + 1 + 12 + 48 = 84\ \mathrm{g/mol} $$

Since both have equal moles and equal molar masses, their masses are also equal.

So, weight percent of $\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$ is

$$ \frac{1}{2} \times 100 = 50\% $$

Answer: Option D, $50\%$

subject: Chemistry
q: 20 mL of a gaseous hydrocarbon was exploded with 120 mL of oxygen. A contraction of 60 mL was observed, and a further contraction of 60 mL took place when KOH was added. What is the formula of the hydrocarbon :
question: 20 mL of a gaseous hydrocarbon was exploded with 120 mL of oxygen. A contraction of 60 mL was observed, and a further contraction of 60 mL took place when KOH was added. What is the formula of the hydrocarbon :
solution:
For a hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$, the combustion reaction is

$$ C_xH_y+\left(x+\frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2+\frac{y}{2}H_2O $$

Given:

  • Volume of hydrocarbon $=20 \text{ mL}$

  • Volume of oxygen taken $=120 \text{ mL}$

  • Contraction after explosion $=60 \text{ mL}$

  • Further contraction with KOH $=60 \text{ mL}$

KOH absorbs $CO_2$, so the further contraction is due to $CO_2$.

So,

$$ \text{Volume of } CO_2 = 60 \text{ mL} $$

Now, $20 \text{ mL}$ hydrocarbon gives $60 \text{ mL}$ carbon dioxide.

So, $1$ volume of hydrocarbon gives $3$ volumes of $CO_2$.

Hence,

$$ x=3 $$

So the hydrocarbon is $C_3H_y$.

Initial total volume:

$$ 20+120=140 \text{ mL} $$

After explosion, contraction is $60 \text{ mL}$.

So volume left after explosion:

$$ 140-60=80 \text{ mL} $$

Out of this, $60 \text{ mL}$ is $CO_2$.

So remaining oxygen:

$$ 80-60=20 \text{ mL} $$

Oxygen used:

$$ 120-20=100 \text{ mL} $$

Thus, $20 \text{ mL}$ hydrocarbon uses $100 \text{ mL}$ oxygen.

So $1$ volume hydrocarbon uses $5$ volumes oxygen.

Therefore,

$$ x+\frac{y}{4}=5 $$

Since $x=3$,

$$ 3+\frac{y}{4}=5 $$

$$ \frac{y}{4}=2 $$

$$ y=8 $$

So the hydrocarbon is

$$ C_3H_8 $$

The correct option is B.

subject: Chemistry
q: 200 ml of an aqueous solution of glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$) has molarity of 0.01 M. Which of the following operations can be done to increase the molarity of solution to 0.015 M?
question: 200 ml of an aqueous solution of glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$) has molarity of 0.01 M. Which of the following operations can be done to increase the molarity of solution to 0.015 M?
options: Evaporate 50 ml of water from this solution
Add 0.18 gm of glucose to solution (assume no change in volume)
solution:
Initial molarity of glucose solution is $0.01\ \text{M}$.

Volume of solution is $200\ \text{mL} = 0.200\ \text{L}$.

So, moles of glucose present are

$$ n = M \times V $$

$$ n = 0.01 \times 0.200 = 0.002\ \text{mol} $$

Now the required molarity is $0.015\ \text{M}$.

Check option A:

If $50\ \text{mL}$ water is evaporated, final volume becomes

$$ 200 - 50 = 150\ \text{mL} = 0.150\ \text{L} $$

Moles of glucose remain the same, $0.002\ \text{mol}$.

$$ M = \frac{0.002}{0.150} = 0.0133\ \text{M} $$

This is not $0.015\ \text{M}$, so option A is not correct.

Check option B:

Molar mass of glucose, $C_6H_{12}O_6$:

$$ 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180\ \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Moles of glucose added:

$$ n = \frac{0.18}{180} = 0.001\ \text{mol} $$

Total moles of glucose become

$$ 0.002 + 0.001 = 0.003\ \text{mol} $$

Volume is assumed unchanged:

$$ V = 0.200\ \text{L} $$

So new molarity is

$$ M = \frac{0.003}{0.200} = 0.015\ \text{M} $$

So option B gives the required molarity.

Options C and D involve adding water, which increases the volume and decreases the molarity, so they cannot be correct.

The correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option B: Add }0.18\ \text{g of glucose to the solution}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: In a textile factory, a double-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a dilute liquor having 4% (by mass) caustic soda to obtain a lye containing 25% solids (by mass). Calculate the mass of water evaporated in the evaporator for every 100 g of feed.
question: In a textile factory, a double-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a dilute liquor having 4% (by mass) caustic soda to obtain a lye containing 25% solids (by mass). Calculate the mass of water evaporated in the evaporator for every 100 g of feed.
solution:
For $100 \text{ g}$ of feed, the weak liquor contains $4\%$ caustic soda by mass.

So, mass of caustic soda in feed is

$$ \frac{4}{100} \times 100 = 4 \text{ g} $$

During evaporation, only water is removed. The amount of caustic soda remains the same.

The final lye contains $25\%$ solids by mass. So, if the final mass of lye is $x$ g, then

$$ 25\% \text{ of } x = 4 $$

$$ \frac{25}{100}x = 4 $$

$$ x = 16 \text{ g} $$

So, the final product weighs $16 \text{ g}$.

Water evaporated is the difference between feed mass and final product mass:

$$ 100 - 16 = 84 \text{ g} $$

So, the weight of water evaporated per $100 \text{ g}$ feed is

$$ \boxed{84.0 \text{ g}} $$

Correct option: Option C.

subject: Chemistry
q: From 250 g of NaCl having 95% purity, what mass (in grams) of $\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}$ with 90% purity can be obtained?
question: From 250 g of NaCl having 95% purity, what mass (in grams) of $\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}$ with 90% purity can be obtained?
solution:
The reaction for preparing sodium sulphate from sodium chloride is:

$$ 2\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\rightarrow \mathrm{Na_2SO_4}+2\mathrm{HCl} $$

Given mass of impure $\mathrm{NaCl}=250\text{ g}$

It is $95\%$ pure, so actual mass of pure $\mathrm{NaCl}$ is:

$$ \frac{95}{100}\times 250=237.5\text{ g} $$

Molar mass of $\mathrm{NaCl}$:

$$ 23+35.5=58.5\text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

Moles of $\mathrm{NaCl}$:

$$ \frac{237.5}{58.5}=4.06\text{ mol} $$

From the balanced equation:

$$ 2\text{ mol NaCl produce }1\text{ mol Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$

So moles of $\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}$ formed:

$$ \frac{4.06}{2}=2.03\text{ mol} $$

Molar mass of $\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}$:

$$ 2(23)+32+4(16)=142\text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

Mass of pure $\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}$ formed:

$$ 2.03\times 142=288.2\text{ g} $$

But the sodium sulphate produced is only $90\%$ pure.

So mass of $90\%$ pure $\mathrm{Na_2SO_4}$ will be:

$$ \frac{288.2\times 100}{90}=320.3\text{ g} $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{320.3\text{ g}} $$

Option D.

subject: Chemistry
q: 1 ml of a gaseous aliphatic compound having molecular formula $C_nH_{3n}O_m$ is burnt completely in excess oxygen. The decrease in total gas volume (in ml) after combustion is
question: 1 ml of a gaseous aliphatic compound having molecular formula $C_nH_{3n}O_m$ is burnt completely in excess oxygen. The decrease in total gas volume (in ml) after combustion is
solution:
For complete combustion of $C_nH_{3n}O_m$:

$$ C_nH_{3n}O_m + \left(n+\frac{3n}{4}-\frac{m}{2}\right)O_2 \rightarrow nCO_2+\frac{3n}{2}H_2O $$

So, $1$ ml of the gaseous compound will require

$$ n+\frac{3n}{4}-\frac{m}{2} = \frac{7n}{4}-\frac{m}{2} $$

ml of oxygen.

After combustion, water vapour condenses to liquid water, so it is not counted in the gaseous volume. The gaseous product left is only $CO_2$.

From $1$ ml of compound, volume of $CO_2$ formed is:

$$ n \text{ ml} $$

Now, contraction in volume is:

$$ \text{Contraction} = \text{volume of compound burnt} + \text{oxygen used} - \text{carbon dioxide formed} $$

$$ = 1+\left(\frac{7n}{4}-\frac{m}{2}\right)-n $$

$$ = 1+\frac{3n}{4}-\frac{m}{2} $$

So, the contraction in volume is

$$ \boxed{1+\frac{3}{4}n-\frac{1}{2}m} $$

Correct option: D.

subject: Chemistry
q: What mass (in grams) of a solute must be dissolved in 100 g of water so that the solution has density 1.2 g/ml and concentration 5% (w/v)?
question: What mass (in grams) of a solute must be dissolved in 100 g of water so that the solution has density 1.2 g/ml and concentration 5% (w/v)?
solution:
Let the mass of solute added be $x$ grams.

Total mass of solution = $(100 + x)$ g

Since density of solution is $1.2$ g/mL, the volume of solution is

$$V = \frac{100 + x}{1.2} \text{ mL}$$

Now, strength is $5\%$ (w/v), which means $5$ g of solute per $100$ mL of solution. So,

$$\frac{x}{V} \times 100 = 5$$

$$\frac{x \times 1.2}{100 + x} \times 100 = 5$$

$$120x = 5(100 + x)$$

$$120x = 500 + 5x$$

$$115x = 500$$

$$x = 4.35 \text{ g}$$

So the correct answer is option D, $4.35$ g.

subject: Chemistry
q: The carbon present in the waste from a nuclear power plant has C12 and C14 in the molar ratio 4 : 1. Find the molecular mass of methane formed using this waste. (Natural abundances: C12 = 98% and C14 = 2%.)
question: The carbon present in the waste from a nuclear power plant has C12 and C14 in the molar ratio 4 : 1. Find the molecular mass of methane formed using this waste. (Natural abundances: C12 = 98% and C14 = 2%.)
solution:
The methane formed from the waste will contain carbon in the same isotopic ratio given for the waste.

The ratio of $^{12}\text{C}$ and $^{14}\text{C}$ is $4:1$ by moles.

So, the average atomic mass of carbon in this sample is

$$ \frac{4 \times 12 + 1 \times 14}{4+1} $$

$$ = \frac{48+14}{5} $$

$$ = \frac{62}{5} $$

$$ = 12.4 $$

Methane is $\text{CH}_4$.

Taking the atomic mass of hydrogen as $1$,

$$ \text{Molecular mass of CH}_4 = 12.4 + 4 \times 1 $$

$$ = 16.4 $$

So, the molecular mass of methane produced is

$$ \boxed{16.4} $$

Correct option: D.

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution has total mass 1200 g and contains 12 g of urea. If the density of this solution is 1.2 g/ml, then the molarity of the solution is
question: A solution has total mass 1200 g and contains 12 g of urea. If the density of this solution is 1.2 g/ml, then the molarity of the solution is
solution:
Mass of urea present $= 12 \text{ g}$

Molar mass of urea, $\text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2$:

$$ = 12 + 16 + 2(14) + 4(1) = 60 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

So, moles of urea:

$$ \text{Moles} = \frac{12}{60} = 0.2 \text{ mol} $$

Now, density of solution is $1.2 \text{ g mL}^{-1}$.

Mass of solution $= 1200 \text{ g}$

So, volume of solution:

$$ \text{Volume} = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Density}} $$

$$ = \frac{1200}{1.2} = 1000 \text{ mL} $$

$$ = 1 \text{ L} $$

Molarity is:

$$ M = \frac{\text{moles of solute}}{\text{volume of solution in litres}} $$

$$ M = \frac{0.2}{1} = 0.2 \text{ M} $$

So, the molarity of the solution is:

$$ \boxed{0.2 \text{ M}} $$

Correct option: A.

subject: Chemistry
q: A water-based solution contains urea and glucose such that their masses are in the ratio 3:1. If the mass ratio of water to glucose present in the solution is 10:1, then the mole fraction of glucose in the solution is
question: A water-based solution contains urea and glucose such that their masses are in the ratio 3:1. If the mass ratio of water to glucose present in the solution is 10:1, then the mole fraction of glucose in the solution is
solution:
Take the mass of glucose as $x$ g.

Given,

$$ \text{mass ratio of urea : glucose} = 3:1 $$

So, mass of urea $= 3x$ g.

Also,

$$ \text{mass ratio of water : glucose} = 10:1 $$

So, mass of water $= 10x$ g.

Now find the number of moles of each substance.

Molar mass of glucose $= 180 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$ n_{\text{glucose}}=\frac{x}{180} $$

Molar mass of urea $= 60 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$ n_{\text{urea}}=\frac{3x}{60}=\frac{x}{20} $$

Molar mass of water $= 18 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$ n_{\text{water}}=\frac{10x}{18}=\frac{5x}{9} $$

Total moles in solution:

$$ n_{\text{total}}=\frac{x}{180}+\frac{x}{20}+\frac{5x}{9} $$

Taking LCM $180$,

$$ n_{\text{total}}=\frac{x}{180}+\frac{9x}{180}+\frac{100x}{180} $$

$$ n_{\text{total}}=\frac{110x}{180} $$

Mole fraction of glucose is

$$ X_{\text{glucose}}=\frac{n_{\text{glucose}}}{n_{\text{total}}} $$

$$ X_{\text{glucose}}=\frac{\frac{x}{180}}{\frac{110x}{180}} $$

$$ X_{\text{glucose}}=\frac{1}{110} $$

So, the correct option is

$$ \boxed{\frac{1}{110}} $$

Option A.

subject: Chemistry
q: A quantity of 1 kg of 1 m glucose solution is diluted to 5 kg. The molality of the diluted solution should be
question: A quantity of 1 kg of 1 m glucose solution is diluted to 5 kg. The molality of the diluted solution should be
solution:
Molality is defined as

$$ m=\frac{\text{moles of solute}}{\text{mass of solvent in kg}} $$

Here, the solution is $1\,m$ glucose solution.

This means:

$$ 1\, \text{mol of glucose is present in } 1\, \text{kg of solvent} $$

But we are given $1\, \text{kg}$ of the solution, not $1\, \text{kg}$ of solvent.

Let the number of moles of glucose in the given solution be $x$.

Since the solution is $1\,m$,

$$ \frac{x}{\text{mass of solvent in kg}}=1 $$

So,

$$ \text{mass of solvent}=x \text{ kg} $$

Molar mass of glucose, $C_6H_{12}O_6$, is

$$ 180\, \text{g mol}^{-1}=0.180\, \text{kg mol}^{-1} $$

So mass of glucose present is

$$ 0.180x \text{ kg} $$

Total mass of solution is $1\, \text{kg}$:

$$ x+0.180x=1 $$

$$ 1.180x=1 $$

$$ x=\frac{1}{1.180}=0.847 \text{ mol} $$

So moles of glucose remain $0.847$ mol even after dilution.

Now the solution is diluted to $5\, \text{kg}$.

Mass of glucose is

$$ 0.180 \times 0.847=0.152 \text{ kg} $$

So mass of solvent in the diluted solution is

$$ 5-0.152=4.848 \text{ kg} $$

Now molality of diluted solution is

$$ m=\frac{0.847}{4.848} $$

$$ m=0.175 $$

So the molality of the diluted solution is

$$ \boxed{0.175\,m} $$

Correct option: D.

subject: Chemistry
q:
100 ml of $\mathrm{PH}_3$ when completely decomposed produces phosphorus and hydrogen. The change in volume of the gas is -

$$ \mathrm{PH}_3(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{~s})+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}) $$
question:
100 ml of $\mathrm{PH}_3$ when completely decomposed produces phosphorus and hydrogen. The change in volume of the gas is -

$$ \mathrm{PH}_3(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{~s})+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}) $$
solution:
From the equation,

$$ \mathrm{PH}_3(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{s}) + \frac{3}{2}\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{g}) $$

We compare only the gaseous volumes, because solid phosphorus does not contribute to gas volume.

According to Gay-Lussac’s law of gaseous volumes, at the same temperature and pressure, gaseous volumes are in the same ratio as their coefficients.

So,

$$ 1 \text{ volume of } \mathrm{PH}_3 \rightarrow \frac{3}{2} \text{ volumes of } \mathrm{H}_2 $$

If $100 \, \text{mL}$ of $\mathrm{PH}_3$ decomposes, then hydrogen formed will be

$$ 100 \times \frac{3}{2} = 150 \, \text{mL} $$

Initial gas volume $= 100 \, \text{mL}$

Final gas volume $= 150 \, \text{mL}$

Change in volume

$$ = 150 - 100 = 50 \, \text{mL} $$

So, the volume increases by

$$ \boxed{50 \, \text{mL}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: Mass of One atom of an element is $=6 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{gm}$ then find the number of moles of atoms in $7.2 \mathrm{Kg}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6 \times 10^{23}\right)$
question: Mass of One atom of an element is $=6 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{gm}$ then find the number of moles of atoms in $7.2 \mathrm{Kg}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6 \times 10^{23}\right)$
solution:
Given:

Mass of one atom $= 6 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{g}$

Mass of sample $= 7.2\,\text{kg} = 7200\,\text{g}$

Avogadro number $N_A = 6 \times 10^{23}$

First, find the molar mass of the element.

We know that mass of one mole of atoms is:

$$ \text{molar mass} = (\text{mass of one atom}) \times N_A $$

So,

$$ = \left(6 \times 10^{-23}\right)\left(6 \times 10^{23}\right)\,\text{g} $$

$$ = 36\,\text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Now number of moles is:

$$ n = \frac{\text{given mass}}{\text{molar mass}} $$

$$ n = \frac{7200}{36} $$

$$ n = 200 $$

So, the number of moles of atoms in $7.2\,\text{kg}$ is

$$ \boxed{200\ \text{mol}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: A compound used in making nylon, is $43.8 \%$ oxygen by mass. There are four oxygen atoms per molecule. What is the molecular weight of compound?
question: A compound used in making nylon, is $43.8 \%$ oxygen by mass. There are four oxygen atoms per molecule. What is the molecular weight of compound?
solution:
If the compound has $4$ oxygen atoms in one molecule, then mass of oxygen in one mole of the compound is

$$4 \times 16 = 64$$

This oxygen makes up $43.8\%$ of the molecular mass.

So, if molecular mass is $M$, then

$$\frac{64}{M} \times 100 = 43.8$$

$$\frac{6400}{M} = 43.8$$

$$M = \frac{6400}{43.8}$$

$$M \approx 146$$

So, the molecular weight of the compound is

$$146 \, \text{u}$$

or

$$146 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

subject: Chemistry
q: A hydrate of magnesium iodide has a formula $\mathrm{MgI}_2 \cdot \mathrm{xH}_2 \mathrm{O}$. If 1.055 g sample is heated to a constant weight of 0.695 g . What is the value of x ?
question: A hydrate of magnesium iodide has a formula $\mathrm{MgI}_2 \cdot \mathrm{xH}_2 \mathrm{O}$. If 1.055 g sample is heated to a constant weight of 0.695 g . What is the value of x ?
solution:
When the hydrate is heated, only water is removed. So:

$$ \text{mass of water lost} = 1.055 - 0.695 = 0.360\ \text{g} $$

Now find moles of anhydrous $\mathrm{MgI_2}$ left behind.

Molar mass of $\mathrm{MgI_2}$:

$$ = 24 + 2(127) = 278\ \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

So moles of $\mathrm{MgI_2}$:

$$ \frac{0.695}{278} = 0.0025\ \text{mol} $$

Now find moles of water lost:

$$ \frac{0.360}{18} = 0.020\ \text{mol} $$

The formula is $\mathrm{MgI_2 \cdot xH_2O}$, so

$$ x = \frac{\text{moles of water}}{\text{moles of } \mathrm{MgI_2}} = \frac{0.020}{0.0025} = 8 $$

So the value of $x$ is

$$ \boxed{8} $$

The hydrate is $\mathrm{MgI_2 \cdot 8H_2O}$.

subject: Chemistry
q: A sample of pure iron is allowed to rust only partly and it forms $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$. After this partial rusting, the mass of the sample increases by $10 \%$. If the fraction of iron that gets converted into $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ is $x$, then find the value of $30x$. $(\mathrm{Fe}=56)$
question: A sample of pure iron is allowed to rust only partly and it forms $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$. After this partial rusting, the mass of the sample increases by $10 \%$. If the fraction of iron that gets converted into $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ is $x$, then find the value of $30x$. $(\mathrm{Fe}=56)$
solution:
Let the initial weight of pure iron be $W$.

Suppose the fraction of iron converted into $\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ is $x$.

So, weight of iron converted is $$ xW $$

Now, in $\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3$,

$$ \text{mass of iron} = 2 \times 56 = 112 $$

$$ \text{mass of oxygen} = 3 \times 16 = 48 $$

So, $112$ g of iron combines with $48$ g of oxygen.

Hence, oxygen gained by $xW$ weight of iron is

$$ \frac{48}{112} \times xW = \frac{3}{7}xW $$

The sample gains $10\%$ of its original weight, so weight gained is

$$ \frac{10}{100}W = \frac{W}{10} $$

This gain is due to oxygen only.

So,

$$ \frac{3}{7}xW = \frac{W}{10} $$

$$ \frac{3}{7}x = \frac{1}{10} $$

$$ x = \frac{7}{30} $$

So,

$$ 30x = 30 \times \frac{7}{30} = 7 $$

Thus, the value of $30x$ is $$ \boxed{7} $$
